title: Discipleship 9: The Role of the Parents in Discipleship, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 9 - The Role of the Parents in Discipleship - Part 1 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Family as the Primary Place for Discipleship
subpoints: Discipleship happens everywhere; parents are the major disciples of children
This section of the series will cover four topics: covenant promises, biblical how-tos, children in public worship, Christian philosophy of education
Matthew 18:1-10 introduces the subject: the importance of children before the Lord and the grave warning against causing them to stumble

outline.title: The Family as an Instituted Government and Culture-Creating Unit
subpoints: The three God-instituted governments: family (Genesis 2), civil government (Genesis 9), and church (Genesis 12)
The family's constitution is the Word of God; the husband is head, wife is chief advisor, children are temporary subjects
Each family is designed to be a distinct culture with its own language, customs, traditions, and unspoken assumptions
The danger of passive parents: not actively establishing a Christian culture allows outside influences to fill the vacuum
The danger of actively rebellious culture: bringing children up under the wrath of God

outline.title: Obstacles to Building a Biblical Culture in the Home
subpoints: The first and greatest obstacle is in the minds of the parents themselves: unrenewed thinking (Romans 12:2)
Reluctance to assume full responsibility; looking to school, church, or sports to do it
Social media, television, sports, ignorance of the Word, child-centered parenting, laziness
The three options for education: public, private, homeschool — none is automatically equivalent to biblical parenting
Fathers must lead; mothers must be convinced of the importance

outline.title: Matthew 18 and the Weight of Parental Responsibility
subpoints: Children in a covenant home are to be treated as believers until they prove otherwise — they are baptized members in a special relationship with God
The disciples' question about greatness answered with a call to childlike humility — not childishness but total dependence on God
Two ways of despising children: neglecting them (pushing them aside for adult concerns) and centering the family on them (child-centered parenting)
Matthew 18:6: the terrifying warning — better a millstone and drowning than to cause a child to stumble
Matthew 18:8-9: the context of maiming is about the seriousness of stumbling children, not self-mutilation

outline.title: Foundation Expectations for Christian Parents
subpoints: First: the Bible is sufficient for fulfilling parental duties (Deuteronomy 6:4-9; 2 Timothy 3:16-17)
Second: discipline is no substitute for regeneration — the goal is not well-behaved children but believing children
Every child is dead in trespasses and sins by nature (Ephesians 2:1-3); strict discipline can only modify behavior, not change hearts
Proverbs 22:6 is a truism (not a promise): whatever you win your children with, that is what you win them for

summary:
This session opens the portion of the Discipleship series dedicated to the parent-child relationship, which Pastor Lyro describes as the primary discipleship relationship in the family after husband and wife. The series will examine four areas: raising children according to God's covenant promises, biblical how-tos of child-rearing, children in public worship, and a Christian philosophy of education. The foundational text is Matthew 18:1-10, where Jesus uses a small child to call his disciples to humility and issues a sobering warning against causing little ones to stumble.

A major theme of this session is the family as an instituted government and culture-creating unit. Just as the civil government and the church government were instituted by God (Genesis 9 and 12 respectively), so the family government was instituted first, in Genesis 2. The family has a constitution (God's Word), a head (the husband), a chief advisor (the wife), and temporary citizens (the children, who are raised to leave and establish their own families). Each family forms a distinct culture with its own vocabulary, customs, and traditions. The practical implication is that parents must be intentional: if they do not actively establish a Christ-centered culture, the world will fill the vacuum. The lesson catalogues common obstacles — social media, television, sports, ignorance, child-centered parenting — and notes that the first and most critical obstacle is the unrenewed thinking of the parents themselves.

The session closes with two foundational expectations for Christian parents. First, the Bible is sufficient for all questions of child-rearing; parents do not need secular psychology to do what God calls them to do. Second, discipline — however necessary and biblical — is no substitute for regeneration. Every child is by nature dead in trespasses and sins (Ephesians 2:1-3). Strict discipline can channel sin in socially acceptable ways, but it cannot change the heart. The goal of Christian parenting is not well-behaved children but believing children, and the gospel must always be the center of the home.
transcript:
## Discipleship Is Happening Everywhere

We continue our series on discipleship, moving now from the husband-wife relationship to the parent-child relationship. As we look at parenting as a discipleship relationship, we will cover four areas: raising children in light of God's covenant promises, the biblical how-tos of child-rearing, children in public worship, and a Christian philosophy of education.

Discipleship is happening everywhere. Our children are being discipled by everything around them. The only question is by what and by whom.

## The Family as the Primary Culture-Making Institution

The first thing I want us to see — and if this is all we grasp today, we will have profited tremendously — is this: **the family must be the primary place where culture is created.**

By "culture" I mean beliefs, worldview, and identity. The family is an instituted government — the first one God established (Genesis 1–2), before civil government (Genesis 9) and before the formal institution of the church (Genesis 12). This government has a constitution: the word of God. The husband is the head of this government. The wife is his chief advisor. The children are the temporary citizens — temporary because parents raise their children to leave and establish their own families.

Each family is its own culture. Every family has its own language, customs, traditions, and countless unspoken assumptions known only to those on the inside. This cultural formation is happening whether parents are intentional about it or not.

Families tend to struggle with two problems. The first is an actively rebellious culture — a home shaped by sin, where children are brought up under the wrath of God. The second, which is far more common in Christian families, is *forgetting that the family is a culture at all* — and allowing outside cultural influences to shape the children by default. When parents abdicate their responsibility to shape their children, someone else will fill the vacuum. And since this is a fallen world, those who rush in will more often than not be fools in the biblical sense.

It is moral idiocy to leave children alone to make their own decisions and form their own values, because everyone else in the world is actively trying to influence them. Children are malleable. They will be shaped either by godly people or ungodly people. You are never the only one shaping your children — the question is whether you are shaping them intentionally for God.

## What Stands in the Way

In our discussion, several obstacles to building a godly culture in the home came up: social media, television, sports, our own hypocrisy, ignorance of the word, laziness, child-centered parenting, misplaced priorities. But I want to suggest that the first and greatest obstacle is within the minds of the parents themselves. The Bible says: *"Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind."* (Romans 12:2) That transformation has to happen first in the parents.

Other obstacles: many parents are reluctant to assume full responsibility for the culture and education of their children. They want to share the responsibility with the church, the school, the soccer team. But while these may provide valuable assistance, the responsibility always and ultimately belongs to the parents. And fathers must lead in establishing this biblical culture at home.

Regarding education: there are three kinds of schooling available to most families — public, private Christian, and homeschool. None of these options automatically equals a Christian education. Simply removing children from the public school is not the same as educating them in Christ. Simply writing a tuition check to a Christian school is not Christian education. And simply keeping children at home is not Christian education either. What matters is whether Christ is truly at the center of the education. We will develop a full Christian philosophy of education in a later lesson.

## Foundation Stones for Christian Parents

**First**: the Bible is sufficient for parenting. Deuteronomy 6 describes an all-encompassing instruction — when you sit, when you walk, when you lie down, when you rise up — in which the word of God governs the entire texture of family life. Paul confirms: *"All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work."* (2 Timothy 3:16–17) If we need the word of God to be equipped for every good work, and raising children is a good work, then the word of God is sufficient for every question parenting will raise.

**Second**: discipline is no substitute for regeneration. Our goal in raising our children is not well-behaved children — it is believing children. Every child, however small and precious, is a sinner from conception:

> *"And you He made alive, who were dead in trespasses and sins... and were by nature children of wrath."* (Ephesians 2:1, 3)

Strict discipline can channel that sin in socially acceptable ways, but it cannot change a heart. Godly, strict discipline must always have a goal that goes beyond good behavior. We need our children to be born again. And our discipline and correction must always be accompanied by the proclamation of the gospel.

## The Warning of Matthew 18

Jesus is urgent and severe in Matthew 18 about how we treat covenant children:

> *"Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in Me to sin, it would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck, and he were drowned in the depth of the sea."* (Matthew 18:6)

The "little lawyer" in our hearts wants to find an exception — "only the ones who already believe." But we raise our children as if they are believers until they prove us otherwise. That is why we baptize them. They are in a special relationship with God as children of believing parents. We treat them as members of the covenant community and press the gospel upon them as belonging to God, not as little pagans who need to be converted from scratch.

The Lord uses this passage on children to make a striking point about humility: unless we are converted and become as little children, we cannot enter the kingdom. A small child cannot do anything for itself; it is entirely dependent. That total reliance on another is what Jesus is calling us to.

Parents: you have kindled a spark in your children that can never be put out. Those children exist forever. How seriously, then, should we take the task of raising them? Verses 8–9 tell us: *so seriously that it is better to be maimed than to cause one of them to stumble*.
